questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: The strength of a conjugate base is determined by the stability of the corresponding conjugate acid. The weaker the conjugate acid, the stronger the conjugate base. In this case, the conjugate acid of acetate ion (CH3COOH) is the weakest, making acetate ion the strongest conjugate base. Similarly, the conjugate acid of sulfate ion (H2SO4) is stronger than that of nitrate ion (HNO3), making sulfate ion a stronger conjugate base than nitrate ion. Chloride ion has the strongest conjugate acid among these ions, making it the weakest conjugate base. The order of decreasing conjugate base is as follows: CH3COO- (Acetate ion) >SO4-2 (Sulfate ion) >NO3-1(Nitrate ion)>Cl-(Chloride ion)

Chloride ion has the strongest conjugate acid among these ions, making it the weakest conjugate base. The order of decreasing conjugate base is as follows: CH3COO- (Acetate ion) >SO4-2 (Sulfate ion) >NO3-1(Nitrate ion)>Cl-(Chloride ion)

The strength of a conjugate base is determined by the stability of the corresponding conjugate acid. The weaker the conjugate acid, the stronger the conjugate base. In this case, the conjugate acid of acetate ion (CH3COOH) is the weakest, making acetate ion the strongest conjugate base. Similarly, the conjugate acid of sulfate ion (H2SO4) is stronger than that of nitrate ion (HNO3), making sulfate ion a stronger conjugate base than nitrate ion. Chloride ion has the strongest conjugate acid among these ions, making it the weakest conjugate base. The order of decreasing conjugate base is as follows: CH3COO- (Acetate ion) >SO4-2 (Sulfate ion) >NO3-1(Nitrate ion)>Cl-(Chloride ion)

The strength of a conjugate base is determined by the stability of the corresponding conjugate acid. The weaker the conjugate acid, the stronger the conjugate base. In this case, the conjugate acid of acetate ion (CH3COOH) is the weakest(partially dissociates and forms equilibrium with both the acid and conjugate base in the solution) making acetate ion the strongest conjugate base. Similarly, the conjugate acid of sulfate ion (H2SO4) is stronger than that of nitrate ion (HNO3), making sulfate ion a stronger conjugate base than nitrate ion. Chloride ion has the strongest conjugate acid among these ions, making it the weakest conjugate base. The order of decreasing conjugate base is as follows: CH3COO- (Acetate ion) >SO4-2 (Sulfate ion) >NO3-1(Nitrate ion)>Cl-(Chloride ion)

questions.explanation: The strength of a conjugate base is determined by the stability of the corresponding conjugate acid. The weaker the conjugate acid, the stronger the conjugate base. In this case, the conjugate acid of acetate ion (CH3COOH) is the weakest(partially dissociates and forms equilibrium with both the acid and conjugate base in the solution) making acetate ion the strongest conjugate base. Similarly, the conjugate acid of sulfate ion (H2SO4) is stronger than that of nitrate ion (HNO3), making sulfate ion a stronger conjugate base than nitrate ion. Chloride ion has the strongest conjugate acid among these ions, making it the weakest conjugate base. The order of decreasing conjugate base is as follows: CH3COO- (Acetate ion) >SO4-2 (Sulfate ion) >NO3-1(Nitrate ion)>Cl-(Chloride ion)

questions.text: Choose which one of the following can not be classed as buffer solution:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Read the main text for explanation.

A buffer solution is an aqueous solution consisting of a mixture of a weak acid and its conjugate base, or vice versa. Its pH changes very little when a small amount of strong acid or base is added to it. HClO4 and NaClO4 is not a buffer solution because both acid and salt are strong.
Read the main text for explanation.
Read the main text for explanation.

questions.explanation: A buffer solution is an aqueous solution consisting of a mixture of a weak acid and its conjugate base, or vice versa. Its pH changes very little when a small amount of strong acid or base is added to it. HClO4 and NaClO4 is not a buffer solution because both acid and salt are strong.

questions.text: Which of the following ions can act both as bronsted acid and base in solvent water?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Read the main text for explanation.
Read the main text for explanation.

CHO3- can act as a Bronsted acid and a Bronsted base. A Bronsted acid is a species that donates a proton (H+), while a Bronsted base is a species that accepts a proton(H+). - In the presence of a stronger base, such as OH-, the H+ ion in CHO3- can be donated to form H2O and CO32-. This reaction makes CHO3- act as a Bronsted acid. - In the presence of a stronger acid, such as HCl, CHO3- can accept an H+ ion to form H2CO3. This reaction makes CHO3- act as a Bronsted base.
Read the main text for explanation.

questions.explanation: CHO3- can act as a Bronsted acid and a Bronsted base. A Bronsted acid is a species that donates a proton (H+), while a Bronsted base is a species that accepts a proton(H+). - In the presence of a stronger base, such as OH-, the H+ ion in CHO3- can be donated to form H2O and CO32-. This reaction makes CHO3- act as a Bronsted acid. - In the presence of a stronger acid, such as HCl, CHO3- can accept an H+ ion to form H2CO3. This reaction makes CHO3- act as a Bronsted base.
hints: both as bronsted acid and base

questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Weak Bronsted bases which are proton acceptors, usually consist of molecules or ions. They react with water, remove a proton from it, and generate OH- ions. Take the examples of NH3: NH3(aq) + H2O(aq) ---> NH4+(aq) + OH- (aq)
Refer to the main text for explanation.

questions.text: The pH of 0.001M aqueous solution of NaOH is:
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Refer to the main text for explanation.
Refer to the main text for explanation.
OH-= 0.001M=10-3 M thus pOH=3 As pH+pOH=14 so pH= 14-3 =11

questions.text: The aqueous solution of which one of the following compounds maintain its pH constant?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Refer to the main text for explanation.
Refer to the main text for explanation.

The solution which maintain pH is called buffer solution. It is prepared from weak acid and its salt with a strong base like CH3COOH/CH3COONa OR weak base and its salt with a strong acid e.g; NH4OH and NH4Cl.
Refer to the main text for explanation.

questions.explanation: The solution which maintain pH is called buffer solution. It is prepared from weak acid and its salt with a strong base like CH3COOH/CH3COONa OR weak base and its salt with a strong acid e.g; NH4OH and NH4Cl.

questions.text: According to the Bronsted-Lowery concept, which of the following species cannot function as an acid?

options.explanation: SO42- has a double negative charge and can't lose more electrons, so it can't act as an acid.

questions.text: Compounds which tend to donate electron pair are known as lewis bases or nucleophile. Which one of the following is not a Lewis base?
